original_hypothesis.title: Forger Off-by-One Allows One Extra Forge Beyond Intended Limit
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` (inclusive), allowing listing when count equals potential. forgeWithListed then increments count without rechecking. A forger with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times: the last listing passes at count=N (N<=N is true), then forge increments to N+1.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The comparison operator should be strict less-than (<) not less-than-or-equal (<=). This off-by-one affects every forger token, allowing one extra forge each. Additionally, forgeWithListed never rechecks the forger's forge potential after listing, compounding the issue when yearly resets occur.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential uses <= instead of <; forgeWithListed line 121 increments without recheck

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Off-by-one: <= instead of < in forge potential check; no recheck at forge time
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: forgingCounts can reach forgePotential+1 for any forger
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing forge potential recheck in forgeWithListed; wrong comparison operator in listForForging

analysis_questions: Trace exact forgingCounts progression for forgePotential=1
Does the yearly reset compound this issue further?
How does the merger check compare (it uses post-increment <= which is correct)?
validation_requirements: Unit test: forge with potential=1 should allow exactly 1 forge, verify it allows 2
Test across year boundary: list, wait 1 year, forge resets, re-forge

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any forger owner with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times. With forgePotential=1: list at count=0 (0<=1 passes), forge->count=1; list at count=1 (1<=1 passes), forge->count=2. Two forges with potential=1. Each extra forge produces an additional NFT entity and earns the forger an extra fee.
prerequisites: Merger willing to pay forging fee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Every forger earns one extra forging fee and produces one extra entity, inflating supply by ~10% (each forgePotential effectively +1)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All forger token holders benefit; dilutes value for all holders

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary test for forge count at exactly forgePotential

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be detected by monitoring forgingCounts vs forgePotential

validation_result.impact_summary: Every forger token can forge forgePotential+1 times instead of forgePotential times, producing one extra entity and earning one extra forging fee per reset period.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `listForForging` function in EntityForging.sol uses `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` (less-than-or-equal) to gate whether a forger can be listed. This allows listing when the count exactly equals the potential. Since `forgeWithListed` increments `forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]` without any recheck against forgePotential (the code comments explicitly say 'do not check forge potential here as it is already checked in listForForging'), the forger gets one extra forge.

Step-by-step for forgePotential=1:
1. forgingCounts=0: listForForging check (0 <= 1) passes → listed
2. forgeWithListed: forgingCounts incremented to 1, no recheck → forge succeeds
3. forgingCounts=1: listForForging check (1 <= 1) passes → listed again
4. forgeWithListed: forgingCounts incremented to 2, no recheck → forge succeeds
5. forgingCounts=2: listForForging check (2 <= 1) fails → cannot list

Result: 2 forges with forgePotential=1. In general, N+1 forges with forgePotential=N.

Notably, the merger's check in `forgeWithListed` uses a post-increment-then-check-with-<= pattern (`forgingCounts[mergerTokenId]++` followed by `forgingCounts[mergerTokenId] <= mergerForgePotential`), which is functionally equivalent to pre-check-with-< and is therefore correct. This confirms the forger check was intended to be strict-less-than.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms the off-by-one. The `<=` operator in listForForging at the forger check, combined with the explicit skip of recheck in forgeWithListed (with code comment confirming intentional skip), creates the defect. The merger's check pattern (post-increment + <=) serves as the correct reference implementation, proving the forger path is wrong.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Uses <= instead of <. When forgingCounts equals forgePotential, this check passes, allowing one more listing (and subsequent forge) than intended.
Increments forger's forgingCounts without rechecking against forgePotential. The comment confirms the intentional omission of the recheck, relying on the flawed listForForging check.

calling_sequence: 1. Forger owner calls listForForging(tokenId, fee) with forgingCounts[tokenId]=N and forgePotential=N
2. _resetForgingCountIfNeeded(tokenId) called - no reset needed if within same year
3. Check: forgePotential > 0 && forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential → N <= N is true, passes
4. Token is listed in listings mapping
6. _resetForgingCountIfNeeded(forgerTokenId) called - no reset if within year
7. forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ increments to N+1 (NO recheck against forgePotential)
8. Merger checks pass (separate logic), new entity minted, fees distributed
9. forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] is now N+1, exceeding forgePotential=N
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: listForForging allows listing at count==potential due to `<=`. forgeWithListed blindly increments the forger count past the limit. The merger's check at lines 125-131 uses post-increment + <= pattern which is correct (equivalent to pre-check with <), confirming the forger's pre-check should use < not <=.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this logic bug. The off-by-one is inherent in the comparison operator.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Any forger token holder can exploit this naturally during normal gameplay. With forgePotential=N:
1. Forge N times normally (counts 0 through N-1)
2. At count=N, list again (N <= N passes)
3. Another user forges with it, count becomes N+1
4. Forger earned N+1 fees and produced N+1 offspring instead of N

No special setup, timing, or capital beyond the normal forging fee is required. Every single forger token in the protocol is affected.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Every forger token can forge one extra time per yearly reset period. For forgePotential=1 tokens, this doubles their forging capacity (2 instead of 1). For forgePotential=9 tokens, it's a ~11% increase. This inflates the entity supply, generates extra forging fees for forger owners, and dilutes the value of all existing tokens. The extra entities also have access to the NukeFund, increasing potential drainage. The bug is systemic and affects all forger tokens from deployment.
recommended_mitigations: Change the comparison in listForForging from `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` to `forgingCounts[tokenId] < forgePotential`
Add a redundant forgePotential check in forgeWithListed before incrementing forgingCounts[forgerTokenId], to defend against stale listings
Consider adding the forgePotential recheck after _resetForgingCountIfNeeded in forgeWithListed to handle edge cases with yearly resets
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts. 2. Mint a forger token with forgePotential=1 (entropy where (entropy/10)%10 == 1 and entropy%3 == 0). 3. Mint a merger token in the same generation. 4. List forger (count=0, 0<=1 passes), forge → count=1. 5. List forger again (count=1, 1<=1 passes), forge with a second merger → count=2. 6. Assert count=2 > forgePotential=1, confirming the violation. 7. Try to list again (count=2, 2<=1 fails) to confirm the boundary.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers may vary slightly depending on formatting, but the logic pattern is clearly confirmed in the provided source
additional_testing_needed: Boundary test: forge count exactly at forgePotential for all forgePotential values 1-9
Integration test: yearly reset + off-by-one compound effect (list at count=potential, wait 1 year, reset to 0, forge again for potential+1 total across years)
Test merger side is NOT affected by the same off-by-one (post-increment + <= is correct)
